Control Arm.evidence: On page 1, in the 'Methods' section, it states: 'Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) using a computerised algorithm and a minimisation technique to either standard of care (androgen deprivation therapy; docetaxel 75 mg/m² intravenously for six cycles with prednisolone 10 mg orally once per day allowed from Dec 17, 2015) or standard of care plus abiraterone acetate...'. On page 3, under 'Randomisation and masking', it further clarifies: 'Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) to either standard of care (control group) or standard of care plus abiraterone...'. This confirms 'standard of care' as the name of the control arm.

Median Follow-Up Duration (mo).value: 96 months (IQR 86–107) in the abiraterone trial and 72 months (61–74) in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial
Median Follow-Up Duration (mo).evidence: Found in the 'Findings' section on page 1, stating the median follow-up for both the abiraterone trial and the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial.

Treatment Arm(s).value: Standard of care plus abiraterone; Standard of care plus abiraterone and enzalutamide
Treatment Arm(s).evidence: On page 1, in the 'Methods' section, it states: 'Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) using a computerised algorithm and a minimisation technique to either standard of care (androgen deprivation therapy; docetaxel 75 mg/m² intravenously for six cycles with prednisolone 10 mg orally once per day allowed from Dec 17, 2015) or standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg (in the abiraterone trial) orally or abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide 160 mg orally once a day (in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial).' The 'Findings' section further clarifies these as 'standard of care plus abiraterone' and 'standard of care plus abiraterone and enzalutamide'.

Control Arm - N.evidence: In the 'Findings' section on page 1, it states: '1003 patients were randomly assigned to standard of care (n=502) or standard of care plus abiraterone (n=501) in the abiraterone trial.' and '916 patients were randomly assigned to standard of care (n=454) or standard of care plus abiraterone and enzalutamide (n=462) in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial.' Figure 1: Trial profile on page 4 also confirms these numbers for the 'standard of care' arms in both trials.

Number of Arms Included.evidence: The 'Methods' section on page 1 states, 'We analysed two open-label, randomised, controlled, phase 3 trials of the STAMPEDE platform protocol'. The 'Findings' section on page 1 details the arms for each trial: the 'abiraterone trial' had 'standard of care' and 'standard of care plus abiraterone' (2 arms), and the 'abiraterone and enzalutamide trial' had 'standard of care' and 'standard of care plus abiraterone and enzalutamide' (2 arms). The 'Randomisation and masking' section on page 3 confirms, 'Each trial randomly assigned patients to its own control group; there were no overlapping controls.' Therefore, there are a total of 4 arms across the two trials (2 control arms and 2 experimental arms).

NCT.evidence: Found in the 'Methods' section on page 1: 'STAMPEDE is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT00268476) and ISRCTN (ISRCTN78818544).' Also confirmed on page 6: 'is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT00268476) and ISRCTN (ISRCTN78818544).'

PubMed ID.evidence: PubMed ID is not explicitly reported in the document.

Trial Name.value: Abiraterone acetate plus prednisolone with or without enzalutamide for patients with metastatic prostate cancer starting androgen deprivation therapy: final results from two randomised phase 3 trials of the STAMPEDE platform protocol
Trial Name.evidence: This is the main title of the article on page 1, which describes the clinical trial.

Author.evidence: Found in the author list on page 1: 'Gerhardt Attard, Laura Murphy, Noel W Clarke, Ashwin Sachdeva, Craig Jones, Alex Hoyle, William Cross, Robert J Jones, Christopher C Parker, Silke Gillessen, Adrian Cook, Chris Brawley, Clare Gilson, Hannah Rush, Hoda Abdel-Aty, Claire L Amos, Claire Murphy, Simon Chowdhury, Zafar Malik, J Martin Russell, Nazia Parkar, Cheryl Pugh, Carlos Diaz-Montana, Carmel Pezaro, Warren Grant, Helen Saxby, Ian Pedley, Joe O'Sullivan, Alison Birtle, Joanna Gale, Narayanan Srihari, Carys Thomas, Jacob Tanguay, John Wagstaff, Prantik Das, Emma Gray, Mymoona Alzouebi, Omi Parikh, Angus Robinson, Amir H Montazeri, James Wylie, Anjali Zarkar, Richard Cathomas, Michael D Brown, Yatin Jain, David P Dearnaley*, Malcolm D Mason*, Duncan Gilbert, Ruth E Langley, Robin Millman, David Matheson, Matthew R Sydes†, Louise C Brown†, Mahesh K B Parmart, Nicholas D James†, for the STAMPEDE investigators‡'.

Year.evidence: Found in the footer of each page (e.g., page 1, 2, 3, etc.): 'Lancet Oncol 2023; 24: 443-56' and 'Vol 24 May 2023'.

Full Pub or Abstract.evidence: The document is extensive, spanning multiple pages with detailed methods, results, discussion, and references, indicating it is a full publication rather than just an abstract.

Phase.evidence: Found in the title of the article on page 1: 'final results from two randomised phase 3 trials'. Also in the 'Methods' section on page 1: 'phase 3 trials of the STAMPEDE platform protocol'.

Original/Follow Up.evidence: The title mentions 'final results' and the 'Summary' and 'Introduction' sections on page 1 and 2 refer to 'long-term outcomes' and 'updated survival data after a median follow-up of 96 months', indicating this is a follow-up report of previously initiated trials.

Treatment Arm 1 Regimen.value: standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg orally
Treatment Arm 1 Regimen.evidence: Found in the 'Methods' section on page 1, under the description of patient randomization and treatment assignment, specifically for the 'abiraterone trial'.

Treatment Arm - N.value: 501 (standard of care plus abiraterone); 462 (standard of care plus abiraterone and enzalutamide)
Treatment Arm - N.evidence: The 'Findings' section on page 1 states: 'Between Nov 15, 2011, and Jan 17, 2014, 1003 patients were randomly assigned to standard of care (n=502) or standard of care plus abiraterone (n=501) in the abiraterone trial.' And 'Between July 29, 2014, and March 31, 2016, 916 patients were randomly assigned to standard of care (n=454) or standard of care plus abiraterone and enzalutamide (n=462) in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial.' These numbers are confirmed in 'Figure 1: Trial profile' on page 4 and the 'Results' section on page 6.

Quality of Life reported.evidence: The document lists primary and secondary outcomes in the 'Outcomes' section on page 4, but 'quality of life' is not mentioned among them or anywhere else in the text.

Quality of Life Scale.evidence: The document extensively details various outcomes such as overall survival, prostate cancer-specific survival, failure-free survival, progression-free survival, metastatic progression-free survival, symptomatic skeletal-related events, toxic effects, adverse events, and cost-effectiveness. However, there is no mention of a 'Quality of Life Scale' or any assessment of participants' overall well-being and satisfaction with their health status using a standardized questionnaire or survey.

Total Participants - N.evidence: On page 1, in the 'Findings' section, it states: 'Between Nov 15, 2011, and Jan 17, 2014, 1003 patients were randomly assigned to standard of care (n=502) or standard of care plus abiraterone (n=501) in the abiraterone trial. Between July 29, 2014, and March 31, 2016, 916 patients were randomly assigned to standard of care (n=454) or standard of care plus abiraterone and enzalutamide (n=462) in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial.' The total number of participants for these two trials is 1003 + 916 = 1919.

Trial.evidence: The title of the article states 'final results from two randomised phase 3 trials of the STAMPEDE platform protocol'. The Methods section also mentions 'two open-label, randomised, controlled, phase 3 trials of the STAMPEDE platform protocol' and that 'STAMPEDE is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT00268476) and ISRCTN (ISRCTN78818544)'.

Median On-Treatment Duration (mo) | Trea.evidence: The document reports median overall survival and median follow-up durations, but does not explicitly state the 'Median On-Treatment Duration (mo)' for the treatment arm.

Median On-Treatment Duration (mo) | Cont.evidence: The document reports median overall survival and median follow-up durations, but does not explicitly state the 'Median On-Treatment Duration (mo)' for the control arm.

Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1.evidence: The 'Methods' section on page 1 describes the randomization for the 'abiraterone trial', where the first experimental arm is 'standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg'. Abiraterone acetate is an androgen biosynthesis inhibitor, and prednisolone is a corticosteroid.

Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | S.evidence: The document describes the study population as patients with metastatic prostate cancer starting androgen deprivation therapy, with stratification based on the presence or absence of distant metastases at the time of random assignment (within 8 weeks). While this population largely aligns with the definition of synchronous metastases, the results are not explicitly reported or stratified by a 'synchronous' prognostic group distinct from 'metastatic disease' in the analyses presented.

Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | M.evidence: The document does not mention or report results based on prognostic groups of patients with metachronous diseases.

Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | H.evidence: The 'Statistical analysis' section on page 5 states: 'Given that metastatic volume is prognostic, outcomes split by low-volume and high-volume disease are of interest and were included as an additional prespecified subgroup analysis'. Table 1 on page 5 includes 'Metastatic volume' with 'High' as a category. Figure 2 (page 7) and Figure 3 (page 8) also present overall survival analysis 'split by low-volume metastatic disease (C, D) and high-volume metastatic disease (E, F)' and 'High-volume disease' respectively.

Reporting by prognostic groups - Y/N | L.evidence: The 'Statistical analysis' section on page 5 states: 'Given that metastatic volume is prognostic, outcomes split by low-volume and high-volume disease are of interest and were included as an additional prespecified subgroup analysis'. Table 1 on page 5 includes 'Metastatic volume' with 'Low' as a category. Figure 2 (page 7) and Figure 3 (page 8) also present overall survival analysis 'split by low-volume metastatic disease (C, D) and high-volume metastatic disease (E, F)' and 'Low-volume disease' respectively.

Primary Endpoint(s).value: overall survival assessed in the intention-to-treat population
Primary Endpoint(s).evidence: On page 1, under the 'Methods' section, it states: 'The primary outcome was overall survival assessed in the intention-to-treat population.' This is further clarified on page 4, under 'Outcomes': 'The primary outcome was overall survival for both trials (defined as the time from randomisation to death from any cause).'

Mode of metastases - N (%) | Synchronous.evidence: The document states that all eligible patients had metastatic, histologically-confirmed prostate adenocarcinoma at baseline (page 1, Methods) and were recruited with distant metastases within 8 weeks of random assignment (page 3, Methods), which aligns with the definition of synchronous metastases. However, the document does not explicitly report the number and percentage (N (%)) of participants with synchronous metastases as a distinct data point in the requested format for the treatment arm.

The document states that all eligible patients had metastatic, histologically-confirmed prostate adenocarcinoma at baseline (page 1, Methods) and were recruited with distant metastases within 8 weeks of random assignment (page 3, Methods), which aligns with the definition of synchronous metastases. However, the document does not explicitly report the number and percentage (N (%)) of participants with synchronous metastases as a distinct data point in the requested format for the control arm.

Mode of metastases - N (%) | Metachronou.evidence: The document states that all eligible patients had metastatic disease at baseline (page 1, Methods), meaning they were already diagnosed with metastases at the time of trial entry. The term 'metachronous metastases' (metastases that occur after the diagnosis of prostate cancer) is not reported or applicable as a baseline characteristic for the recruited trial population. Therefore, no number or percentage for metachronous metastases in the treatment arm is reported.

The document states that all eligible patients had metastatic disease at baseline (page 1, Methods), meaning they were already diagnosed with metastases at the time of trial entry. The term 'metachronous metastases' (metastases that occur after the diagnosis of prostate cancer) is not reported or applicable as a baseline characteristic for the recruited trial population. Therefore, no number or percentage for metachronous metastases in the control arm is reported.

Secondary Endpoint(s).value: prostate cancer-specific survival (defined as the time from randomisation to death from prostate cancer); failure-free survival (defined as biochemical failure, local or lymph node progression, distant metastases, or death from prostate cancer); progression-free survival (defined as failure-free survival but excluding biochemical failure); metastatic progression-free survival (defined as progression of or new distant metastases or death from prostate cancer); symptomatic skeletal-related events (defined as pathological fracture or spinal cord compression or bone pain requiring radiotherapy or surgery); toxic effects and adverse events; and cost-effectiveness of research treatment.
Secondary Endpoint(s).evidence: The 'Outcomes' section on page 4 explicitly states: 'Secondary outcomes were prostate cancer-specific survival (defined as the time from randomisation to death from prostate cancer); failure-free survival (defined as biochemical failure, local or lymph node progression, distant metastases, or death from prostate cancer); progression-free survival (defined as failure-free survival but excluding biochemical failure); metastatic progression-free survival (defined as progression of or new distant metastases or death from prostate cancer); symptomatic skeletal-related events (defined as pathological fracture or spinal cord compression or bone pain requiring radiotherapy or surgery); toxic effects and adverse events; and cost-effectiveness of research treatment.'

ORR - N (%) | Treatment | SD.evidence: The document focuses on overall survival, prostate cancer-specific survival, failure-free survival, metastatic progression-free survival, and adverse events. It does not report Stable Disease (SD) as N (%) for the treatment arm.

ORR - N (%) | Treatment | PD.evidence: The document mentions 'Disease progression was the most common reason reported for permanently stopping treatment (202 [41%] of 498 in the abiraterone trial vs 151 [34%] of 445 stopping either treatment in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial; appendix pp 6, 22)' on page 11. However, this refers to the reason for stopping treatment, not the overall percentage of patients with Progressive Disease (PD) in the treatment arm as a response outcome.

However, this refers to the reason for stopping treatment, not the overall percentage of patients with Progressive Disease (PD) in the control arm as a response outcome.

COE_RCT_IND_OVERALL_RJ.evidence: The document is a report on two randomized controlled trials (RCTs) within the STAMPEDE platform protocol. It describes the methods, findings, and interpretation of these trials. It does not provide an 'overall risk judgment of individual criteria of evidence (COE) in randomized controlled trials (RCT)' as it is a primary research article, not a systematic review or meta-analysis assessing the quality of other RCTs.

No. of Deaths - N | Treatment.evidence: Found in Figure 1: Trial profile on page 4, under the 'Abiraterone trial' section, for the 'standard of care plus abiraterone' arm, it states '293 died'. This is further confirmed on page 10 in the 'Discussion' section which mentions '231 (79%) of 293 with addition of abiraterone were attributed to prostate cancer'.

No. of Deaths - N | Control.evidence: Found in Figure 1: Trial profile on page 4, under the 'Abiraterone trial' section, for the 'standard of care' arm, it states '371 died'. This is further confirmed on page 10 in the 'Discussion' section which mentions '325 (88%) of 371 deaths with standard of care'.

Add-on Treatment.value: Abiraterone acetate plus prednisolone; Abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide
Add-on Treatment.evidence: The 'Methods' section on page 1 states: 'Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) ... to either standard of care ... or standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg (in the abiraterone trial) orally or abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide 160 mg orally once a day (in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial).' The 'Background' section on page 1 also mentions: 'Abiraterone acetate plus prednisolone (herein referred to as abiraterone) or enzalutamide added at the start of androgen deprivation therapy'.

Treatment Class.value: Androgen Deprivation Therapy (ADT) and its combinations with abiraterone and/or enzalutamide
Treatment Class.evidence: The document describes the study as investigating "abiraterone acetate plus prednisolone with or without enzalutamide for patients with metastatic prostate cancer starting androgen deprivation therapy" (Page 1, Title). The treatment arms are categorized as "standard of care (androgen deprivation therapy)", "standard of care plus abiraterone acetate and prednisolone", and "standard of care plus abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide" (Page 1, Methods; Page 3, Randomisation and masking). This indicates that the primary treatment class is Androgen Deprivation Therapy, with additional agents forming intensified regimens.

However, the median progression-free survival values in months for the overall population in the treatment arm are not explicitly reported in the text or any tables.

Type of Therapy.value: The clinical trial provided several types of treatment: 1. Standard of care, which included androgen deprivation therapy (luteinising hormone-releasing hormone agonists or antagonists or bilateral orchiectomy) and, after December 17, 2015, docetaxel (75 mg/m² intravenously for six cycles) with prednisolone (10 mg orally once per day). 2. Standard of care plus abiraterone acetate (1000 mg) and prednisolone (5 mg) orally once a day. 3. Standard of care plus abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide (160 mg) orally once a day.
Type of Therapy.evidence: The description of the treatment arms is found in the 'Methods' section on page 1 and further detailed in the 'Procedures' section on page 3. Specifically, page 1 states: 'Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) ... to either standard of care (androgen deprivation therapy; docetaxel 75 mg/m² intravenously for six cycles with prednisolone 10 mg orally once per day allowed from Dec 17, 2015) or standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg (in the abiraterone trial) orally or abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide 160 mg orally once a day (in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial).' Page 3, 'Procedures' section, elaborates on standard of care: 'The standard of care was androgen deprivation therapy with luteinising hormone-releasing hormone agonists or antagonists or bilateral orchiectomy; after implementation of a protocol amendment (version 13; Dec 17, 2015), docetaxel (75 mg/m²) intravenously for six cycles with prednisolone (10 mg) orally once per day were permitted after starting androgen deprivation therapy but before starting study treatment.'

PFS Rate (%) | Overall | Treatment.evidence: A single overall progression-free survival (PFS) rate (percentage) for the treatment arm is not explicitly reported in the document. Hazard ratios comparing treatment to control are provided, but not a standalone percentage rate for the treatment arm.

PFS Rate (%) | Overall | Control.evidence: A single overall progression-free survival (PFS) rate (percentage) for the control arm is not explicitly reported in the document. Hazard ratios comparing treatment to control are provided, but not a standalone percentage rate for the control arm.

Adverse Events - N (%) | All-Cause Grade.evidence: Found in the 'Findings' section on page 1: 'In the first 5 years of treatment... the highest toxic effects were seen when abiraterone and enzalutamide were added to standard of care (302 [68%] of 445...)' This refers to all-cause grade 3-5 toxic effects in the treatment arm of the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial.

Found in the 'Findings' section on page 1: 'In the first 5 years of treatment... the highest toxic effects were seen when abiraterone and enzalutamide were added to standard of care (302 [68%] of 445 vs 204 [45%] of 454 with standard of care).' This refers to all-cause grade 3-5 toxic effects in the control arm of the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial.

Adverse Events - N (%) | Treatment-relat.evidence: The document reports all-cause grade 3-5 toxic effects but does not provide a general count or percentage for treatment-related adverse events of grade 3 or higher for the treatment arm.
The document reports all-cause grade 3-5 toxic effects but does not provide a general count or percentage for treatment-related adverse events of grade 3 or higher for the control arm.

Found in the 'Discussion' section on page 11: 'Cardiac causes were the most common cause of death due to adverse events (five [1%] with standard of care plus abiraterone and enzalutamide [two attributed to treatment]...).' This indicates 2 treatment-related Grade 5 (death) events in the treatment arm of the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial. A specific percentage for these 2 events is not provided.

The document mentions 'one (<1%) with standard of care in the abiraterone trial' for cardiac causes of death, but it does not explicitly state that this event was attributed to treatment in the control arm. Therefore, treatment-related Grade 5 for the control arm is not found.

Median OS (mo) | Overall | Treatment.evidence: The document reports median overall survival for the treatment arm in two separate trials: 76.6 months for the abiraterone trial and 73.1 months for the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial (page 1, 'Findings' section). A single meta-analyzed median overall survival for the overall population across both trials is not explicitly reported.

Median OS (mo) | Overall | Control.evidence: The document reports median overall survival for the control arm in two separate trials: 45.7 months for the abiraterone trial and 51.8 months for the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial (page 1, 'Findings' section). A single meta-analyzed median overall survival for the overall population across both trials is not explicitly reported.

Median OS (mo) | High volume | Treatment.evidence: Found in Figure 3, Panel E ('High-volume disease'), 'Overall' row, under the 'Median overall survival, months (95% CI)' column for 'Study drug treatment': 54 (47-65).

Median OS (mo) | High volume | Control.evidence: Found in Figure 3, Panel E ('High-volume disease'), 'Overall' row, under the 'Median overall survival, months (95% CI)' column for 'Standard of care': 37 (34-42).

Median OS (mo) | Low volume | Treatment.evidence: Found in Figure 3, Panel D ('Low-volume disease'), 'Overall' row, under the 'Median overall survival, months (95% CI)' column for 'Study drug treatment': 97 (92-NR).

Median OS (mo) | Low volume | Control.evidence: Found in Figure 3, Panel D ('Low-volume disease'), 'Overall' row, under the 'Median overall survival, months (95% CI)' column for 'Standard of care': 65 (58-76).

OS Rate (%) | Overall | Treatment.value: Abiraterone trial: 48% (95% CI 43-52) at 84 months; Abiraterone and enzalutamide trial: approximately 40% at 84 months
OS Rate (%) | Overall | Treatment.evidence: For Abiraterone trial, found in the last paragraph of 'Results' section on page 9: 'At 84 months in the abiraterone trial, overall survival was ... 48% (43-52) with standard of care plus abiraterone.' For Abiraterone and enzalutamide trial, derived from Figure 2B (Overall survival (%) vs Time since randomisation (months)) on page 7, where the 'Standard of care plus abiraterone plus enzalutamide' curve is approximately 40% at 84 months, which is the maximum follow-up shown for this trial.
